text: The surgeon begins the preperitoneal repair by dissecting the fat around the falciform ligament and making an initial incision into the peritoneum, avoiding the retromuscular space. Visible scarring from previous procedures is noted, and there is some bleeding during the dissection. The surgeon works in areas with ample fat to prevent peritoneal holes and aims to develop a large peritoneal pocket for mesh placement to address a two-centimeter defect.

The surgeon places an 11-centimeter circular mesh into the preperitoneal space, creating a pocket superiorly and dissecting around the hernia inferiorly. The dissection is facilitated by the presence of preperitoneal fat, avoiding fenestrations in the peritoneum. The surgeon approaches what appears to be the hernia, noting its proximity to the site of a previous 5mm trocar used for a VP shunt.

The surgeon reduces the hernia using robotic instruments, revealing the actual hernia that necessitated the procedure. The surgical field shows mostly pre-peritoneal fat, and the reduction is performed without manual assistance at the bedside.

The surgeon reduces the incarcerated hernia and proceeds to take down adhesions to create a working space. The robotic approach allows for precise visualization and management of bleeding from peri-umbilical vessels while developing a pocket for the 11 by 11 cm mesh.

The surgeon encounters difficulty developing the contralateral peritoneal flap but eventually progresses, maintaining the transversalis fascia while pushing the peritoneum down. There is some oozing around vessels, which is controlled using COAG and bipolar instruments. The surgeon continues to create a broad pocket, aiming for at least four to five centimeters of circumferential coverage for the mesh around the defect.

The surgeon continues to develop the surgical space, improving visualization of the pocket. The larger defect is closed transversely, with the suture locked in place. The closure integrates the smaller defect using the same suture, working from the contralateral side towards the ipsilateral side. The scrub tech assists with reducing the umbilical skin for incorporation into the closure, resembling an umbilicalplasty. The fascia is tightened to complete the repair.

The surgeon uses a permanent V-lock suture to close a smaller defect in a vertical orientation, integrating it into the closure of another hole. After securing the closure with additional bites, the surgeon places a Bard soft mesh into the expanded pocket, ensuring it sits flat.

The surgeon places the Bard soft mesh with the sticky side facing up for fixation in a ventral hernia repair. The mesh is centrally fixated at two or three spots without peripheral fixation, ensuring it is not overly tight to avoid deformation.
The surgeon ties three knots to secure the V-lock suture after breaking it, successfully closing the flap and reducing the hernia.
